Visit the stunning Markazi Jamia Mosque When it comes to architecture, the Markazi Jamia Mosque is one of the highlights of Rawalpindi. It was completed at the beginning of the 20th century, and it’s a beautiful example of Islamic architecture. Before this mosque was built, there were around 80 small mosques in Rawalpindi, but none of them were large

metadata.title: 8 Best Things to Do in Rawalpindi, Pakistan (2026) - Laure Wanders
metadata.description: So, if Rawalpindi is a city you'd like to explore, this post is for you. You will find the best things to do in Rawalpindi, along with insider tips and

text: mosque was built, there were around 80 small mosques in Rawalpindi, but none of them were large enough for the Friday prayer or Eid gatherings. This is why plans were made to build a larger mosque, and in 1903, Shah Muhammad Ayub, the exiled prince of Afghanistan, laid the foundation stone of the Markazi Jamia Masjid. With its vibrant colours and intricate tilework, this is one of the most beautiful places to visit in Rawalpindi, and it’s one of my favourites. 🧕 Make sure to dress modestly and

Explore the spiritual Hazrat Shah Chan Charagh Shrine This Sufi shrine is the final resting place of Hazrat Shah Chan Charagh, one of the propagators of Islam in this region. He lived in the 18th century and is remembered as a

text: one of the propagators of Islam in this region. He lived in the 18th century and is remembered as a spiritual leader who spread the message of peace and tolerance. Sufism is the mystical branch of Islam which emphasises meditation, mantras and prayers, and it’s definitely worth visiting a Sufi shrine when you’re in Pakistan. These are different from mosques, and the Hazrat Shah Chan Charagh is pretty interesting. 👀 The area where this shrine is located is great for a stroll as it’s full of

Shop at Raja Bazaar The bustling Raja Bazaar is one of Rawalpindi’s oldest bazaars. It’s 2 km (1,2 mi) long, and you will find plenty of shops selling anything from electronics to clothing and shoes here. This bazaar was named after Raja Sherbaz, a local landlord who donated the land it’s built on, and it’s a great place to soak up the city’s atmosphere. On top of

See the Kalyan Das Temple Photo credit: Shiraz Hassan The construction of this Hindu temple started in the 1850s and was completed in 1880. Like most Hindu temples, it was abandoned when Hindus left the region after the partition of India in 1947. Although the Kalyan Das Temple still stands today, it’s in an abominable condition.

text: India in 1947. Although the Kalyan Das Temple still stands today, it’s in an abominable condition. Nevertheless, this is one of Rawalpindi’s most important historical buildings, and its main structure still stands. The interior is in shambles, though. When you’re in the street where this temple can be found, you will find the Government Qandeel Secondary School for visually impaired children. The Kalyan Das Temple is located in its courtyard behind its main gate. 🔒 Note that you will need

Explore the little streets Exploring the small streets and alleys is one of my personal favourite things to do in Rawalpindi. This part of the city reminded me of Lahore, which is one of my favourite cities. You will find plenty of

The best place to get lost in the little streets is in the area around the Hazrat Shah Chan Charagh Shrine (#2 on this list). 6. This includes weapons, uniforms, tanks, ammunition, and more. But this museum also showcases the detailed history of the Pakistani Army, and it includes a gallery dedicated to the war on terrorism. Autumn (September to October): You can expect temperatures between 25°C and 30°C (77°F and 86°C) during autumn. This is a good time to visit Rawalpindi. text: Rickshaw: This is my favourite way to get around South Asian cities. Rickshaws are affordable and efficient. Private taxi: Although a private taxi is more comfortable, it’s also more expensive, and you might get stuck in traffic. Metrobus: Taking the metrobus is the cheapest way to get around Rawalpindi, and it’s good to know that it connects this city to Islamabad. 🛺 I recommend downloading the Careem app when you’re in Pakistan. Where to Stay in Islamabad Most tourists stay in Islamabad and not Rawalpindi, as most of the hotels here don’t host foreigners. You will find my top recommendations below. The 108 Hotel (⭐ 8.9/10, $$$): The 108 is one of the best hotels in Islamabad. The rooms are elegant and they have a nice restaurant and terrace. Riviera Courtyard Guest House (⭐ 8.9/10, $$): This beautiful
